data.title: Vera Bradley: A Colorful Turnaround
data.description:
Vera Bradley (VRA) is not the first company that shot itself, and probably is not the last one. In 2024, the former CEO announced brand transformation and basically started to pivot from Vera Bradley's core identity. That upset the loyal customers, slashed sales, and led the stock to an all-time low. Although that transformation was not the only misstep, it was the final nail in the coffin.

In June 2025, the former CEO stepped down, and Ian Bickley was appointed as interim CEO and later as permanent CEO. Martin Layding was also appointed as CFO and COO, with Ivan Brockman as a member of the board. Bickley's retail experience in handbags and accessories combined with Layding's experience in PE-backed companies and Brockman's experience in investment banking suggests that the goal is to turn around the company and could even be to sell it.

The sale is also in the interest of the new management team due to the change in control terms in their contracts. Their options will be immediately vested (instead of a three-year vesting period), and they might walk away with up to 1.5x of their base salary as cash severance and pro-rated bonuses. However, even if the company is not sold, a successful turnaround could still offer a great opportunity.

Right after taking charge, Bickley introduced Project Sunshine to turn the company back to its core identity:

Over the past quarter, we have remained focused on delivering Project Sunshine which anchors on reclaiming Vera Bradley's joyful optimism and acts as our North Star, bringing creative energy to how we work within functions and across the Vera Bradley team. It is leading us to new ideas for products, marketing and channels, transforming how we work with each other and encouraging us to think differently about our operations.

At the same time, we have been addressing past missteps with urgency and implementing comprehensive changes across the business and organization, demonstrating the focus and agility of our team. To remind you, the 5 strategic pillars under Project Sunshine are: first, sharpening our brand focus through product relevance and storytelling. Second, resetting our go-to-market approach with data-led insights. Third, rewiring our digital ecosystem across all touch points. Fourth, implementing Outlet 2.0 for a more brand-enhancing retail experience. And fifth, reimagining how we work with new capabilities and organizational alignment.

To understand what the main goal of the turnaround is, we should start by understanding the core business.

Background

The fascinating story of Vera Bradley and its cofounders, Barbara Bradley and Pat Miller, can be found in the book "A Colorful Way of Living" and in a short documentary video of "The Vera Bradley Story." I also recommend watching this interview with Barbara Bradley.

This is basically how the company was created, by sewing patterns on a ping-pong table with an initial $500 of borrowed money:

While traveling on vacation in March of 1982, neighbors and friends Patricia R. Miller and Barbara Bradley Baekgaard were awaiting a flight in Atlanta when they noticed a lack of colorful, distinctive luggage.These dynamic women turned $500 into a company named after Barbara’s mother to design, manufacture and market colorful, high-quality handbags, luggage and accessories for women.

The cofounders have always wanted to give more color to life and make it more fun and joyful for people around them. This can be seen not only in Vera Bradley bags and accessories but also in their hotel and Breast Cancer Foundation. This attitude created a unique company culture and brought exceptionally loyal customers.

During the 2008 financial crisis, Vera Bradley was one of the few outperformers and was able to sustain 24% annual growth with great margins. In 2010, the company went through an IPO and was listed on Nasdaq. This is the financial performance of the business since its IPO:

Of note is that Vera Bradley acquired 75% of Pura Vida for $75M in 2019 and bought the remaining 25% for $10M in 2023. This turned out to be a horrible acquisition that increased costs and made the business unprofitable. Pura Vida was sold in 2025 for a total consideration of $3.5M (page 73 of the latest 10-K). Although the loss on their investment was significant, this divestiture was necessary to turn the company back to profitability.

After Vera Bradley went public, I think there were two major causes that eventually led the company to its current situation:

  1. The growth pressure and short-term thinking to hit revenue targets, which is always a challenge on Wall Street, resulted in overexpansion in products and stores. This overexpansion led management to introduce discounting cycles and basically train their customers to never pay the full price.
  2. As the company grew and cofounders got older, their control over business operations faded, which eventually resulted in losing the unique culture, brand identity, and loyal customers.

Now the question is, is there any hope for a successful turnaround?

Turnaround

As mentioned, there are 5 strategic pillars that the current management team has been pursuing. While it is still too soon to see the results of their actions on the financial results, I think there are some signs of improvement, especially in the efforts to sharpen the brand focus. They discontinued many of their products with a minimalist look and simplified SKUs. They also started to focus on storytelling in social media and increased brand awareness. As a result, the "Vera Bradley" search trend has shown positive growth over the last year:

Looking at the "Vera Bradley" search trend and VRA stock price since its IPO, it seems like the Google search trend has usually moved before the stock price and hence acted as a leading indicator.

Comparing FY2026 with FY2025, net revenues declined by 24% in Q1, 24.6% in Q2, 11.7% in Q3, and 1.7% in Q4. This clearly shows that momentum was created after the shift in company strategy. Gross margin declined more than 12% in Q3 (42% vs 54.5%) and improved by around 2% in Q4 (47.8% vs 45.9%). The decline in gross margin at the beginning of Project Sunshine was due to heavy reliance on clearance to sell the discontinued products. However, the management was able to stop the bleeding in Q4 and returned the company to profitability after 1.5 years.

Despite the strategic shift, the current return to profitability was mainly attributed to cost management. In FY2026, management reduced corporate expenses by $10.6M. However, revenue continued to decline in both direct and indirect segments.

One could argue that this decline is mainly due to intense competition and a change in customer taste. While these are some of the likely causes, I believe mismanagement was the primary reason. It would not be unexpected to see stabilized or even growing revenues with proper management. Additionally, the operating income of the core business shows why Vera Bradley can be an attractive target for a PE. By further reducing corporate expenses and focusing on the brand's core identity, the company can offer a decent ROI.

Another strategy that the new management is currently trying to implement is the scarcity sales model. For instance, look at this Facebook post for selling only 50 handcrafted bags:

I think this is a great idea that can change sentiment around the brand.

Valuation

Vera Bradley is currently trading below its book value. The company has no outstanding debt, $76M in inventory, and $18.5M in cash. It also owns its HQ property and a distribution center (around 430,000 square feet). The HQ was valued at $25M and listed for sale-leaseback in 2025 (the listing was later terminated). While the liquidation value of inventory is probably less than its book value and there are some costs associated with termination of the outstanding leases, I think it is fair to say that the company is currently trading close to its liquidation value. Therefore, just stopping cash burn can probably move the stock up.

Valuing a company in the middle of a turnaround is like art. The current numbers are usually awful, which can easily justify their current low valuation or even further slump. However, in case they can come back to growth and even partially return to the good old days, the upside is certainly huge. This, of course, takes time, and that is why it is usually too soon to jump in until it is too late. For instance, you can see what happened in Fossil Group (FOSL). Everything was looking awful for a few years, and then the new management got on board and started to fix things. However, the stock was still performing poorly for over a year until everything started to look better and the stock almost quintupled in a very short period of time.

The way I like to play this kind of situation is to stay on the sidelines before seeing some signs of improvement. This can push the stock up significantly and shrink the margin of safety, but these companies often experience a pullback or another round of pessimism that can give us what we want at a price we want. Vera Bradley has already shown some signs of improvement, and that is why the stock went up around 50% in less than a month after its earnings. Therefore, I believe we are in the third stage, which is waiting for a good entry point. If that opportunity never arrives, I would rather move on and go to the next opportunity than jump into something with an inadequate margin of safety.

Looking at the total revenues and margins in the last five years, I think a successful turnaround will easily bring up sales to $300M with total operating income of around $10M in two to three years. Since they have NOL from the Pura Vida divestiture, the operating income will be close to their net income. Assuming P/E = 10 and adding $18M of cash, the stock price would sit around $4.2 per share.

(($10M x 10) + $18M) / 28M Shares Outstanding = $4.2

I want at least a 15% return over 3 years. Therefore, the price at which I am comfortable jumping in is:

$4.2 / (1.15)^3 = $2.8

You can play around with any of those assumptions, but I think the overall approach is making sense for turnaround stories. As mentioned, it is very possible we never see that price again, but if we do, that would be the kind of opportunity I like.

Risk

The main problem with turnarounds is they usually fail! Many things have to work out in favor of the business to see a successful one. That is why it is important to have an adequate margin of safety. One thing I especially focus on in a turnaround thesis is the quality of management and whether they have similar experience in the past. In Vera Bradley, I think the current team is appropriate for the task, which increases the chance of success.

Competition is an important barrier that can challenge the operation. I think if management sticks to the current plan and focuses more on a scarcity model, they can bring back their loyal customers and find new ones. Storytelling is also key for this brand, and the current management seems to understand it well.

Conclusion

Vera Bradley has already shown some signs of improvement and indication of a successful turnaround. They have the right people in charge, a good story to tell, and unique and nostalgic products to sell. These might offer a great opportunity. However, anything can be a good investment at the right price and a horrible investment at the wrong price. I would wait for a price at which I think it is absolutely right to jump in. Until then, I will stay on hold!

data.title: Outlook For Copper As Supply And Demand Disrupted
data.description:
The Middle East conflict is raising fresh concerns about global growth while putting commodities and supply chain vulnerabilities back in the spotlight. Craig Hutchison, Director, Equity Research with TD Cowen, looks at what investors should watch in mining as demand cools, logistics face disruption, and pricing dynamics grow more uncertain.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie: Well, the conflict in the Middle East has had a wide-ranging impact on the commodities market. And it's not just supplies of oil and natural gas being disrupted. Joining us now to discuss what it means for base metals like copper is Craig Hutchison, Director for Equity Research with TD Cowen. And for full disclosure on the companies covered by TD Cowen, please see the link at the end of this video. Craig, great to have you on the show again.

Craig Hutchison: Yeah, thanks for having me back.

Anthony Okolie: All right, so let's talk about the Middle East and the impact it's having on base metals mining space. What are you seeing there?

Craig Hutchison: Yeah. Volatile times we live in. Obviously, the war is having a major impact on energy supply. Energy supply is critical for global growth. So if you constrain that supply or the prices go up, as we've seen at the pump, and I'm sure everyone's experiencing it, that's going to be a headwind to global growth.

At the same time, there are other factors that I'm sure we can talk about it, some of the other impacts to supply itself. Sulfuric acid has been constrained. That's a major input to the extraction of copper. There's been a few operations in the Middle East that have also been constrained. The Middle East is not a major producer of copper. Iran has some small mines. But overall, there have been some offsetting factors that have helped the market be a little bit balanced in this environment.

Anthony Okolie: What about diesel? What's the sensitivity there?

Craig Hutchison: Yeah. So we put out a piece on Friday, our Q1 preview, and we surveyed all the companies that we cover and asked them the question, what's the impact in the current price environment? And, by and large, most of the companies have assumed between $60 and $80 WTI or Brent at the start of the year. So every $10 move in the diesel price -- or the oil price, I should say -- has about a $0.02 to $0.05 impact on the cost structure.

It depends on where the mines are located, depends on the type of mine. So, for example, Capstone, which has open-pit mines, which, as you can imagine, rely heavily on diesel for the movement of trucks and shovels, they're probably looking at somewhere around $0.03 a pound for every $10 move. But, on average, you're probably looking at, at today's prices, maybe about a $0.10 to $0.15 impact on cash costs.

There are some offsetting factors as well. Most of the companies produce some precious metals, whether it's gold or silver. And the precious metal prices have obviously moved very high. And for the most part, guides are assuming around $3,900 per ounce gold prices. And today, we're sitting at $4,700, $4,800 an ounce. So those benefits from the higher revenues in gold and silver will actually offset a lot of the impact on diesel. But it definitely is a cost headwind.

Anthony Okolie: Now, obviously, with higher prices, it's going to have an impact on the global economy and, of course, global growth. So how will those headwinds for global growth impact the supply and demand for copper?

Craig Hutchison: Yeah. So we rely heavily on Wood Mackenzie, which is a subscription we pay for, which follows the industry. And they've reset their expectations already to reflect the higher energy prices. So going into the war, they were expecting around 2.6% demand growth. Now they've ratcheted that down to around 2.2%.

So on their numbers, we have, I would say, a fairly balanced market -- about a 300-ton deficit, which, in the context of around a 27 million ton market, is fairly balanced, but slightly on the deficit side. Interestingly, and maybe we could talk about just the Ivanhoe situation as well, there have been some disruptions on the supply side, which have actually impacted the numbers further. So it's actually made the market a little bit tighter. I think the market's probably closer to 400,000 tons of deficit at this point.

Anthony Okolie: OK. So, given that backdrop, what's the health of the miners right now? And what are you expecting in the upcoming earnings season?

Craig Hutchison: Yeah. So Q1, I think, is going to be a softer quarter. There are seasonal factors that drive the lower production this quarter. We saw similar seasonality in 2025. So seasonality could be weather-related impacts. Obviously, we've had a tough winter here in the Northeast, so guys who have operations in the northern climates have experienced a lot of snow, and that impacts production.

Grade profiles -- Q1, for whatever reason, this year round, and similar to last year, has lower grades. And as you get into higher grades, you get higher production. Other factors would just include ramp-ups. Teck has their QB mine that's getting better and better every day. Capstone had a strike, and one of their biggest mines is ramping up as well. So you'll see stronger production throughout the year.

But going into Q1, I think it'll be the softest quarter from a production standpoint. But at the same time, we also have record copper prices and gold prices in the quarter. So earnings should be strong. Balance sheets, for most of the producers, are all in good shape. We've gone through a period here of nice earnings for the last couple of years, so no one's in a liquidity crisis. But we'll see stronger, I think, earnings and production growth as we come out of Q1.

Original Post

data.title: HumanX 2026 AI Conference Takeaways: The Gap Is Widening
data.description:
By Zeno Mercer

I just got back from HumanX, the enterprise AI conference held last week in San Francisco. Now in its second year, HumanX has already hit a remarkable milestone: over 6,500 attendees and hundreds of speakers, ranging from Al Gore, to Fei-Fei Li (widely known as the godmother of AI), and Daniela Rus, director of MIT’s CSAIL and strategic advisor for the ROBO Global Index suite.

Zeno Mercer (front right) at Daniela Rus’ (center) more intimate roundtable discussion that occurred right after her panel “Building AI Systems That Sense, Decide, and Act” at HumanX 2026.

The Biggest Takeaway?

That there are now two types of companies in the world. The first treats headcount and agentic workflows + token usage/optimization as equally important inputs into how the business runs. The second is still in discovery, without a real AI strategy. The gap between them is widening by the quarter.

To leaders across the world, whether it’s executive leadership, government, academic institution, or nonprofit, it is time to lock in. Others already have.

If your AI strategy is rolling out Gemini or Copilot seats and tracking license utilization, you are measuring the wrong thing. The step change is not seat count. It is whether your organization knows how to design, deploy, and manage agentic workflows, including for roles that were previously considered “non-technical”.

Look at how Jack Dorsey is rebuilding Block (XYZ) as an AI-first company. As an internal AI agent that now handles roughly 90% of code submissions, headcount came down 40%, and guidance went up. That is what an operating model built around agents looks like, not a dashboard counting chat prompts. It is also the direction that we will eventually see the majority of companies head - industry and context depending - over the coming years.

The Big 3 Takeaways

  1. Trust (and budget, and access to compute) is the bottleneck, not capability. Twenty-three panels centered on the disconnect between what AI can already do and what organizations are willing to let it do. Procurement decisions worth billions are being made without adequate evaluation frameworks. Ion Stoica of the LMSYS team named it the “trust gap” on the reliability panel. Only about 40% of AI vendors are making it into production at Fortune 500 companies today. This is a governance and cultural problem, not a technical one.
  2. Agents crossed from demos to production. Agentic AI appeared across 79 panel links. The production signals are concrete. Vercel reports 30% of deployments are now driven by agents with 167% year-over-year growth. Ramp’s CTO said 60% of pull requests merged in the prior week came from their coding agent. Tom Eggemeier, CEO of Zendesk, said his top customer resolves 92% of customer interactions through an AI agent. Uber’s CTO Praveen Naga pegged AI-generated code at roughly 70% of new commits. Salesforce is calling agents the “digital workforce” with intent.
  3. Physical AI emerged as the next wave. NVIDIA’s Jensen Huang explicitly framed physical AI as “wave three” of this cycle. Multiple panels covered world models (Runway, World Labs, Black Forest Labs), sim-to-real transfer, and autonomous systems that extend well past self-driving. Skild AI presented an omnibodied-intelligence thesis: one model, any body type. As the research lead covering Robotics and AI for the ROBO Global Robotics and Automation Index (ROBO) and the ROBO Global Artificial Intelligence Index (THNQ), I dare say this is the thread that most directly compounds the next decade.

Governance vs Speed, the P&G / Harvard Study Leaders Cannot Ignore

Seth Cohen of Procter & Gamble presented a study that P&G ran with Harvard Business School across 700 employees. Four groups (teams, teams with AI, individuals, individuals with AI) were each asked to generate new product innovation ideas, blind-judged by experts.

The teams who had access to AI performed the best. But a very close second was not one of the teams without access to AI. It was the individuals who had access to AI. The teams and individuals without AI came in a very distant third and fourth place.

Read that again. A single person with AI beat an entire team without AI.

Which is precisely why governance-as-drag is now a strategic risk. Typeface’s research, cited on the Creativity at AI Speed panel, found that 67% of marketers miss cultural moments because of slow review and approval cycles. On a separate panel, enterprise buyers described hard usage caps with approval queues attached, and the admission that “you’re punishing your performers.”

The lesson for leaders: governance is necessary, but governance that kneecaps your best individual contributors goes beyond strategic self-sabotage, to potentially existential, organizational apoptosis off the world scale.

So - what are the companies that are using AI worried about?

A Deeper Dive into the State of Compute

The clearest moment of the week came from Dr. Fei-Fei Li on the opening night mainstage. Bloomberg’s Ed Ludlow asked how much of World Labs’ fresh one billion dollar raise went to Jensen Huang. Her answer was an instant: “And Lisa, right?”

The room laughed. It was one of the sharper two-word summaries of the compute market all week. Frontier model companies are not single-vendor buyers. NVIDIA (NVDA) and AMD are both in every serious build order. Sumit Sadana of Micron (MU) framed it the same way on the Silicon Shift panel.

Albeit pre-recorded from a week prior, Jensen chimed in with his own take (which was also the framing of an entire panel series), which is that the industry is a five-layer stack: power, chips, infrastructure, models, applications.

Bryan Catanzaro of NVIDIA, on the “5 Layer Cake” panel, added that there are now four scaling laws driving compute demand simultaneously: pre-training, post-training, deployment-time reasoning, and agents. All four are energy-bounded.

Hunger Games for Compute

From Nebius (NBIS), a sovereign AI cloud provider and THNQ constituent that has been pulling significant hyperscaler partnership flow, to Super Micro Computer (SMCI), a THNQ holding and a key hardware partner for xAI’s Colossus build-out in Memphis, the demand curve for training and increasingly inference compute (what we can fairly call “the token economy”) was visible in every side conversation. Rodrigo Liang of SambaNova put it plainly: “hyperscalers, neoclouds, sovereign clouds, large-scale enterprises” are all running data centers for token factories now.

The Token Economics

On pricing, Sam Altman was repeatedly cited (by other speakers, on the Business Reality of AI Adoption panel, among others) as the clearest voice signaling that usage-based, metered pricing is where consumer and enterprise AI is going. The economic logic is simple. Power is scarce. Flat-rate pricing in a power-scarce world is unsustainable for the provider. Enterprise sales teams at some companies are already consuming 1B tokens per month. Together AI, speaking on the open-vs-closed panel, reported growth from 10 billion tokens per day in early 2025 to over 5 trillion tokens per day now. Three orders of magnitude in roughly a year on a single platform.

Jensen put the new unit of economic output in one phrase: “tokens per watt.” Every data center has to optimize for it. Every architecture has to be co-designed for it. This also, from my perspective, expands into local, open-source AI that can run on everyday hardware, similar to how the personal AI “Openclaw economy” is taking off - I’ll go as far as to say it’s a combination of the PC and smartphone moment for AI.

Matt Garman, CEO of AWS (AMZN), opened the agentic-inflection panel on the twentieth anniversary of AWS (Pi Day). His framing: agents are how most enterprises will capture most of their AI value, and AWS is building agent-first infrastructure accordingly.

Part II: The Agentic Economy

Beyond pure inference, a large slice of HumanX was builders focused on the agentic layer itself. Agentic harnesses, token optimization, and what one speaker called the “battlefield of the future” for enterprise workflow.

Speed matters (sub-second tool calls, inference optimized for throughput not latency, as Impala AI’s cofounder put it: “we treat it like an HPC problem, not a web service”). Databases matter (Snowflake (SNOW), Datadog (DDOG), and the data plane plays). Agentic web search is a new category of its own, with Bright Data, Tavily, You.com, Brave Search, Exa, and Perplexity all competing to be the default retrieval layer for AI agents. For others, it’s not speed, but cost (especially as we see gigantic data loads for multi-modal physical AI training).

The trust stack sits underneath all of it. Palo Alto Networks (PANW) and CrowdStrike (CRWD) for enterprise security. Cloudflare (NET) running stablecoin rails for agent-to-agent payments, which is early but real.

Bottom Line Takeaways

Jensen put the unit of economic output in one phrase: tokens per watt. Every operator in the room was already pricing around it. In a power-scarce world, metered usage is the only honest way to sell it.

In robotics and physical AI, the metric expands. You still track tokens per watt, and you also have to answer for useful actions per joule, sensor fidelity per dollar, actuation cycles before drift, manipulations per fault, and uptime per operator. The same discipline, pushed through the reality of physics: friction, latency, heat, and power constraints that no model can pretend away once it is driving a body.

That is why this new cycle holds together at the index level. Digital AI and Physical AI are two substrates of the same story. ROBO and THNQ sit across the stack it runs on: the silicon and photonics that turn watts into useful cycles, the clouds and sovereign compute that meter them, the data and trust layers that keep them legible, and the agents and world models that turn capacity into outcomes.

Disclaimers

ROBO is the underlying index for the ROBO Global Robotics & Automation ETF (ROBO), the L&G ROBO Global Robotics and Automation UCITS ETF (ROBO.LN), and the Global X ROBO Global Robotics & Automation ETF (ROBO.AU). THNQ is the underlying index for the ROBO Global Artificial Intelligence ETF (THNQ) and the L&G Artificial Intelligence UCITS ETF (AIAI.LN).

Disclosure: © VettaFi LLC 2026. All rights reserved. This material has been prepared and/or issued by VettaFi LLC ("VettaFi") and/or one of its consultants or affiliates. It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit www.vettafi.com
